papers.title: Dynamical evolution of star-forming regions: III. Unbound stars and predictions for Gaia
papers.author_names: Christina Schoettler Richard J. Parker Becky Arnold Liam P. Grimmett Jos de Bruijne Nicholas J. Wright

abstract.text: We use $N$-body simulations to probe the early phases of the dynamical evolution of star-forming regions and focus on mass and velocity distributions of unbound stars. In this parameter space study, we vary the initial virial ratio and degree of spatial and kinematic substructure and analyse the fraction of stars that become unbound in two different mass classes (above and below 8 M$_{\\\\odot}$). We find that the fraction of unbound stars differs depending on the initial conditions. After 10 Myr, in initially highly subvirial, substructured simulations, the high-mass and lower-mass unbound fractions are similar at $\\\\sim$23 per cent. In initially virialised, substructured simulations, we find only $\\\\sim$16 per cent of all high-mass stars are unbound, whereas $\\\\sim$37 per cent of all lower-mass stars are. The velocity distributions of unbound stars only show differences for extremely different initial conditions. The distributions are dominated by large numbers of lower-mass stars becoming unbound just above the escape velocity of $\\\\sim$3 km s$^{-1}$ with unbound high-mass stars moving faster on average than lower-mass unbound stars. We see no high-mass runaway stars (velocity > 30 km s$^{-1}$) from any of our initial conditions and only an occasional lower-mass runaway star from initially subvirial/substructured simulations. In our simulations, we find a small number of lower-mass walkaway stars (with velocity 5-30 km s$^{-1}$) from all of our initial conditions. These walkaway stars should be observable around many nearby star-forming regions with Gaia.

triage.label: rule-direct
triage.reason: Direct title match: unbound, escaping, or ejected stars

brief.tldr: [research paper] N-body simulations reveal that initial virial ratio and substructure significantly influence the fraction of unbound stars in star-forming regions: subvirial, substructured regions produce similar unbound fractions (~23%) for high- and low-mass stars after 10 Myr, while virialised, substructured regions show a stark asymmetry—only ~16% of high-mass stars become unbound versus ~37% of low-mass stars. Unbound stars are predominantly low-mass objects escaping at velocities just above 3 km s⁻¹ (walkaway stars), with no high-mass runaway stars (>30 km s⁻¹) observed; these walkaway stars are detectable via Gaia's kinematic surveys and offer a direct observational probe of early dynamical evolution in star-forming regions.

source_fields.title: Dynamical evolution of star-forming regions: III. Unbound stars and predictions for Gaia

source_fields.venue: Monthly notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Methods: The paper proposes a simulation method that generates star-forming regions with realistic initial conditions—using a Maschberger initial mass function and fractal spatial and velocity structures—where stars are distributed according to a fractal dimension $D$ to model substructure and turbulence, and their velocities are set to reflect observed velocity dispersion trends in molecular clouds. Results: The cumulative 2D-velocity distributions show that initially substructured regions ($D \\leq 2.0$) undergo violent relaxation, leading to velocity increases that are most pronounced in highly subvirial ($\\alpha_{\\rm{vir}} < 0.5$) conditions, while less substructured regions ($D \\geq 2.6$) only collapse when initially subvirial, with velocity evolution converging toward virial equilibrium by 10 Myr across all initial virial ratios. Discussion: Star-forming regions with initial substructure evolve rapidly kinematically, producing significantly higher unbound fractions—especially of low/intermediate-mass stars—and yielding detectable runaways only in initially subvirial and substructured regions, with unbound fractions and velocity distributions showing strong dependence on initial conditions, though high-mass stars remain largely bound and do not reach runaway velocities under typical conditions. Conclusions: N-body simulations show that while cumulative velocity distributions are insensitive to initial conditions, unbound star fractions—especially high-mass runaway or walkaway stars—depend on initial structure and dynamics, with low/intermediate-mass walkaways being common and suggesting observational biases in current fast-star detections. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel method to distinguish between monolithic and hierarchical star formation by analyzing the initial spatial substructure of star-forming regions, addressing the problem of how early dynamical evolution obscures the true formation history of clusters and associations.

abstract: We find that the fraction of unbound stars differs depending on the initial conditions.
In initially virialised, substructured simulations, we find only $\\\\sim$16 per cent of all high-mass stars are unbound, whereas $\\\\sim$37 per cent of all lower-mass stars are.

The distributions are dominated by large numbers of lower-mass stars becoming unbound just above the escape velocity of $\\\\sim$3 km s$^{-1}$ with unbound high-mass stars moving faster on average than
These walkaway stars should be observable around many nearby star-forming regions with Gaia.
title: Unbound stars and predictions for Gaia

catalog_assessment.evidence: Purely theoretical N-body simulation study. Abstract and TLDR discuss simulated populations, not observational data of real stars. Mentions Gaia detectability as a prediction.

papers.title: Stellar escapers from M67 can reach solar-like Galactic orbits
papers.author_names: T. G. J{\\\\o}rgensen R. P. Church

abstract.text: We investigate the possibility that the Sun could have been born in M67 by carrying out $N$-body simulations of M67-like clusters in a time-varying Galactic environment, and following the galactic orbits of stars that escape from them. We find that model clusters that occupy similar orbits to M67 today can be divided up into three groups. Hot clusters are born with a high initial $z$-velocity, depleted clusters are born on cold orbits but are destroyed by GMC encounters in the Galactic disc, and scattered clusters are born on cold orbits and survive with more than 1000 stars at an age of 4.6 Gyr. We find that all cluster models in all three cluster groups have stellar escapers that are kinematicaly similar to the Sun. Hot clusters having the lowest such fraction $f_{\\\\odot} = 0.06$ %, whilst depleted clusters have the highest fraction, $f_{\\\\odot} = 6.61$ %. We calculate that clusters that are destroyed in the Galactic disc have a specific frequency of escapers that end up on solar-like orbits that is $\\\\sim$ 2 times that of escapers from clusters that survive their journey.

brief.tldr: [research paper] N-body simulations of M67-like open clusters in a time-varying Galactic potential reveal three distinct evolutionary pathways: hot (high initial z-velocity), depleted (destroyed by GMC encounters), and scattered (survive with >1000 stars). All groups produce stellar escapers with solar-like kinematics, with depleted clusters yielding the highest fraction (6.61%) and hot clusters the lowest (0.06%), while surviving clusters produce escapers at ~2× the rate of destroyed ones, supporting the hypothesis that the Sun could have originated in M67 via tidal stripping and dynamical scattering.

source_fields.title: Stellar escapers from M67 can reach solar-like Galactic orbits

source_fields.venue: Monthly notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Results: Cluster A exhibits a high fraction of stellar escapers ($f_{\\odot} \\sim 40\\%$) that reach solar-like Galactic orbits, with significant star loss during four closely spaced GMC encounters (at ~520 Myr and ~820 Myr) and a stable half-mass radius of ~5.5 pc, followed by a gradual decline to ~3.8 pc due to ongoing tidal and internal dynamics. Discussion: The Milky Way model reproduces observed velocity dispersions $\\sigma_U$ and $\\sigma_V$ well but underestimates $\\sigma_W$, leading to a higher fraction of stellar escapers in solar-like orbits ($f_{400} = 3.6\\%$) compared to Gustafsson et al. (1.8%), due to a weaker vertical potential and larger initial birth radii, while the ROSO model with a less top-heavy GMC distribution yields lower velocity dispersions and $f_{400} = 2.1\\%$. Conclusions: The evolution of M67-like clusters in a galactic potential reveals three distinct groups—hot, depleted, and scattered—differing in their initial dynamics and GMC interactions, with scattered and depleted clusters producing more stellar escapers on solar-like orbits, indicating that destroyed clusters contribute twice as many such escapers compared to surviving ones. Introduction: This paper presents a novel $N$-body simulation approach to test the hypothesis that the Sun was born in the M67 cluster by demonstrating that tidal stripping from spiral arms and giant molecular clouds can scatter M67 to high galactic heights without destroying the outer solar system, thereby resolving the contradiction between M67's high altitude and the survival of the outer solar system. Milky Way Model: The paper proposes a detailed Milky Way model that incorporates an axisymmetric potential, two spiral arms composed of inhomogeneous oblate spheroids, a Galactic bar modeled as a prolate spheroid, and Giant Molecular Clouds distributed along the spiral arms, using pre-calculated gravitational forces and specific density laws to simulate the dynamical structure and evolution of the galaxy. $N$-body simulations: N-body simulations were conducted to model the escape histories of 16 M67 candidates by incorporating realistic galactic tidal forces and significant GMC encounters, using a modified nbody6tt code with a tidal tensor and backward-integrated GMC orbits. Cluster survival and M67-like orbits: M67-like orbits are defined using observational data and integrated backward in time to determine key orbital parameters, and among 25,000 test particles in a Milky Way model, only 37 match M67's orbit within one standard deviation, with survival over 4.6 Gyr depending on GMC encounters and tidal disruption.

abstract: investigate the possibility that the Sun could have been born in M67 by carrying out $N$-body simulations of M67-like clusters in a time-varying Galactic environment, and following the galactic orbits of stars

Hot clusters are born with a high initial $z$-velocity, depleted clusters are born on cold orbits but are destroyed by GMC encounters in the Galactic disc, and scattered clusters are born on cold orbits
and survive with more than 1000 stars at an age of 4.6 Gyr.

catalog_assessment.evidence: N-body simulation study of cluster evolution and stellar escapers. Focus is on theoretical modeling of the Sun's potential origin in M67, not on presenting observational data for a catalog of real escapers.

papers.title: Origin and Status of Low-Mass Candidate Hypervelocity Stars
papers.author_names: Bum-Suk Yeom Young Sun Lee Jae-Rim Koo Timothy C. Beers Young Kwang Kim

abstract.text: We present an analysis of the chemical abundances and kinematics of six low-mass dwarf stars, previously claimed to be candidate hypervelocity stars (HVSs). We obtained moderate-resolution ($R\\\\sim$ 6000) spectra of these stars to estimate their abundances of several chemical elements (Mg, Si, Ca, Ti, Cr, Fe, and Ni), and derived their space velocity components and orbital parameters using proper motions from \\\\gaia\\\\ Data Release 2. All six stars are shown to be bound to the Milky Way, and in fact are not even considered high-velocity stars with respect to the Galactic rest frame. Nevertheless, we attempt to characterize their parent Galactic stellar components by simultaneously comparing their abundance patterns and orbital parameters with those expected from various Galactic stellar components. We find that two of our program stars are typical disk stars. For four of the program stars, even though their kinematic probabilistic membership assignment suggests membership in the Galactic disk, based on their distinct orbital properties and chemical characteristics, we cannot rule out the exotic origins of these objects, as follows. Two stars appear to be runaway stars from the Galactic disk. One program star has possibly been accreted from a disrupted dwarf galaxy or dynamically heated from a birthplace in the Galactic bulge. The last object may be either a runaway disk star or has been dynamically heated. Spectroscopic follow-up observations with higher resolution for these curious objects will provide a better understanding of their origin.

triage.label: rule-direct
triage.reason: Direct title match: hypervelocity stars, high-velocity star surveys or candidates

brief.tldr: [research paper] The paper re-examines six low-mass dwarf stars previously flagged as hypervelocity stars (HVSs) using moderate-resolution spectroscopy and Gaia DR2 proper motions. Despite their high space velocities, all stars are found to be bound to the Milky Way, refuting their HVS status. Chemical abundance patterns and orbital parameters reveal that two are typical disk stars, while four exhibit anomalous kinematics and compositions suggesting exotic origins: two are runaway disk stars, one may have originated from a disrupted dwarf galaxy or bulge, and the last is ambiguous—possibly a runaway or dynamically heated object. The study highlights the limitations of kinematic-only HVS searches and underscores the need for chemical abundance analysis to constrain stellar origins in Galactic components.

source_fields.title: Origin and Status of Low-Mass Candidate Hypervelocity Stars

source_fields.sections_text: Footnotes: This section lists citations and references to online tools, databases, and institutions used in the research, including links to astronomical data resources and software. Discussion: The study identifies several stars with non-canonical disk properties—such as high eccentricities or large vertical excursions—indicating origins as accreted dwarf galaxy remnants, dynamically heated disk stars, or runaway stars, with SDSS J113102.87+665751.1 being a high-eccentricity bulge-origin star and SDSS J172630.60+075544.0 possibly a heated thick-disk or runaway star, based on combined chemical and dynamical analyses. Conclusions: The six low-mass dwarf stars previously identified as high-velocity stars by Pal14 are actually bound to the Milky Way, with their high velocities due to erroneous proper motion assignments; while most belong to the thin or thick disk, some exhibit exotic orbital and chemical properties suggesting possible origins such as accretion from dwarf galaxies or dynamical ejection. Introduction: This paper presents a novel identification and characterization of low-mass hypervelocity star candidates from large spectroscopic surveys, addressing the unresolved problem of the origin of hypervelocity stars by expanding the search beyond massive B-type stars to include cooler, lower-mass dwarfs that may provide critical insights into the dynamical ejection mechanisms and the total mass of the Milky Way. Spectroscopic Observations And Reduction: Spectroscopic observations of six HVS candidates and eight comparison stars were obtained using the DIS spectrograph at Apache Point Observatory, reduced through standard procedures, and analyzed to derive stellar parameters and chemical abundances with a resolving power of R = 6000. Kinematic And Chemical Properties Of Our Program Stars: The kinematic properties of the program stars, derived from Gaia DR2 proper motions and SSPP distances, indicate that most are likely bound to the Milky Way, with discrepancies in earlier studies attributed to misidentifications or inconsistent proper motion measurements. Estimation Of Stellar Parameters And Chemical Abundances: Stellar parameters and chemical abundances were estimated using the SP_Ace code, which employs a library of equivalent widths from synthetic spectra to minimize χ² via the Levenberg-Marquadt method, with red channel spectra preferred due to better coverage of the wavelength range.

abstract: We present an analysis of the chemical abundances and kinematics of six low-mass dwarf stars, previously claimed to be candidate hypervelocity stars (HVSs).

We obtained moderate-resolution ($R\\\\sim$ 6000) spectra of these stars to estimate their abundances of several chemical elements (Mg, Si, Ca, Ti, Cr, Fe, and Ni), and derived their space velocity components
All six stars are shown to be bound to the Milky Way, and in fact are not even considered high-velocity stars with respect to the Galactic rest frame.
We find that two of our program stars are typical disk stars.
Two stars appear to be runaway stars from the Galactic disk. One program star has possibly been accreted from a disrupted dwarf galaxy or dynamically heated from a birthplace in the Galactic bulge.
title: Origin and Status of Low-Mass Candidate Hypervelocity Stars

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper presents analysis of six specific low-mass dwarf stars. Uses moderate-resolution spectra (chemical abundances) and Gaia DR2 proper motions/parallaxes to derive space velocities and orbital parameters. Provides object-level observational data for a defined sample.

papers.title: Gaia Stellar Kinematics in the Head of the Orion A Cloud: Runaway Stellar Groups and Gravitational Infall
papers.author_names: K. V. Getman E. D. Feigelson M. A. Kuhn G. P. Garmire

abstract.text: This work extends previous kinematic studies of young stars in the Head of the Orion A cloud (OMC-1/2/3/4/5). It is based on large samples of infrared, optical, and X-ray selected pre-main sequence stars with reliable radial velocities and Gaia-derived parallaxes and proper motions. Stellar kinematic groups are identified assuming they mimic the motion of their parental gas. Several groups are found to have peculiar kinematics: the NGC 1977 cluster and two stellar groups in the Extended Orion Nebula (EON) cavity are caught in the act of departing their birthplaces. The abnormal motion of NGC 1977 may have been caused by a global hierarchical cloud collapse, feedback by massive Ori OB1ab stars, supersonic turbulence, cloud-cloud collision, and/or slingshot effect; the former two models are favored by us. EON groups might have inherited anomalous motions of their parental cloudlets due to small-scale `rocket effects' from nearby OB stars. We also identify sparse stellar groups to the east and west of Orion A that are drifting from the central region, possibly a slowly expanding halo of the Orion Nebula Cluster. We confirm previously reported findings of varying line-of-sight distances to different parts of the cloud's Head with associated differences in gas velocity. Three-dimensional movies of star kinematics show contraction of the groups of stars in OMC-1 and global contraction of OMC-123 stars. Overall, the Head of Orion A region exhibits complex motions consistent with theoretical models involving hierarchical gravitational collapse in (possibly turbulent) clouds with OB stellar feedback.

triage.label: rule-weak-llm-confirmed
triage.reason: Weak title match: runaway-related mechanisms LLM confirmed: Title explicitly mentions 'Runaway Stellar Groups' and abstract discusses stellar groups departing birthplaces with abnormal motions, consistent with runaway star phenomena

brief.skipped_reason: weak rule match; retained with search metadata only

source_fields.venue: Monthly notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Results: The analysis reveals distinct kinematic subgroups in the Head of the Orion A cloud, including a distance and velocity gradient toward OMC-1/4, higher $V_X$ velocities west of the cloud, and a clear north-south $V_Z$ gradient, with specific groups like NGC 1977 and EON-north/south showing unique motions indicating possible runaway stellar groups or gravitational infall. Discussion: Stellar groups in the Head of the Orion A cloud exhibit complex, non-uniform kinematics, with NGC 1977 and related groups showing significant velocity discrepancies (~3 km s⁻¹) and outward motion (~3 pc Myr⁻¹), indicating ongoing gravitational infall and dispersal, best explained by hierarchical collapse or OB star feedback rather than simple turbulence or collisions. Conclusions: This study uses a large sample of young stars with precise kinematic data to confirm gravitational infall and identify distinct stellar groups with peculiar motions in the Orion A cloud head, revealing complex dynamical structures and supporting the presence of ongoing contraction in key molecular regions. Introduction: This paper makes the novel contribution of systematically analyzing the distance and kinematics of young stars in the northern Head of Orion A, demonstrating that their velocities closely track the parental molecular gas, providing strong evidence for the continued kinematic inheritance of stellar motion from their filamentary birth environment and addressing the problem of how star formation preserves the large-scale dynamics of molecular clouds. Samples And Methods: The paper proposes a comprehensive method to select a reliable sample of young stars in the Orion A region by cross-matching Gaia DR2 data with multiple existing young stellar catalogs, applying astrometric and parallactic filters to ensure high-quality distance and kinematic measurements. Appendix A Three catalog tables: This appendix presents three tables detailing catalog data for young stars in Orion A, including Gaia astrometric properties, multi-wavelength photometry, extinction, age estimates, and kinematic velocities, enabling comprehensive analysis of stellar populations and their associations. Appendix D Visualizing Core-Halo Age Gradient In ONC: This section demonstrates the presence of a core-halo age gradient in the Orion Nebula Cluster, with younger stars concentrated in the core (near the Trapezium) and older stars distributed in the halo, a pattern consistent across different age estimation methods and supporting hierarchical star formation models. Appendix C Raw spatial maps of distance and velocities across the Head of Orion A: This section presents raw spatial maps of stellar distances and velocity components across the Head of Orion A, complementing smoothed kinematic maps by including individual stars and excluding outliers to enhance interpretability. Appendix B Transformation of proper motions and radial velocities to $V_{X}$, $V_{Y}$, and $V_{Z}$: This section details the transformation of Gaia-derived proper motions and radial velocities into three-dimensional stellar velocities $V_X$, $V_Y$, and $V_Z$ by correcting for perspective effects, center-of-mass motion, and projecting onto the sky plane, while accounting for the reference radial velocity $V_{rad,0}$.

abstract: This work extends previous kinematic studies of young stars in the Head of the Orion A cloud (OMC-1/2/3/4/5).
It is based on large samples of infrared, optical, and X-ray selected pre-main sequence stars with reliable radial velocities and Gaia-derived parallaxes and proper motions.

The abnormal motion of NGC 1977 may have been caused by a global hierarchical cloud collapse, feedback by massive Ori OB1ab stars, supersonic turbulence, cloud-cloud collision, and/or slingshot effect;
EON groups might have inherited anomalous motions of their parental cloudlets due to small-scale `rocket effects' from nearby OB stars.
Three-dimensional movies of star kinematics show contraction of the groups of stars in OMC-1 and global contraction of OMC-123 stars.

catalog_assessment.evidence: Study based on large samples of pre-main sequence stars with reliable radial velocities, Gaia parallaxes, and proper motions. Identifies stellar kinematic groups, including runaway groups, using observational astrometric and radial velocity data.
